name: Duat
description: The Egyptian underworld where souls are judged
significance: Duat is a significant sacred site in ancient tradition, serving as an important location for worship and spiritual practice.
icon:
<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round">
  <path d="M5 21h14"/>

geography: Described as a vast underground realm with multiple levels and chambers; contains the Hall of Two Truths where judgement occurs; filled with obstacles, demon guardians, and dangerous passages; also contains fields (Aaru) where the righteous dwell eternally
relatedEvents: Opening of the Mouth ceremony enabling entry
Weighing of the Heart against feather of Ma'at
Journey through 12 hours of night (Book of the Dead)
Osiris's resurrection and reign as judge of the dead
accessibility: mythical - Accessible only after death with proper burial rites and magical knowledge (Book of the Dead); requires passing trials and proving virtue before Forty-Two Judges

creatures.name: Anubis (god of mummification and the dead)

keyMyths.title: The Weighing of the Heart
keyMyths.description: The deceased's heart is weighed against the feather of Ma'at (truth and justice). If the heart is lighter, the soul can proceed to the afterlife; if heavier, it is devoured by Ammit, the Devourer of Souls, resulting in eternal oblivion. This judgement determines the fate of the soul in the Duat.

keyMyths.title: Ra's nightly journey through the Duat
keyMyths.description: Each night, the sun god Ra travels through the Duat in his solar barque, battling the serpent Apophis and other forces of chaos. His successful journey ensures the sunrise and the continuation of order, requiring the assistance of various deities and the souls of the blessed dead.

keyMyths.title: Osiris as Ruler of the Duat
keyMyths.description: After being murdered by his brother Set and resurrected by Isis, Osiris became the ruler of the Duat and judge of the dead. He presides over the Hall of Judgement, overseeing the weighing of the heart ceremony and determining the fate of souls.

keyMyths.title: The Trials and Challenges of the Duat
keyMyths.description: The deceased must navigate a treacherous landscape filled with demons, traps, and magical obstacles to reach the Hall of Judgement. These trials test their worthiness and require them to possess knowledge of specific spells and passwords to overcome them.

keyMyths.title: The Lake of Fire
keyMyths.description: A fiery lake within the Duat serves as a place of purification or punishment for souls deemed unworthy. Some interpretations suggest it is a transitional space where souls are cleansed before proceeding to the afterlife, while others see it as a place of eternal torment.

sources.name: The Egyptian Book of the Dead
sources.description: A collection of spells, hymns, and illustrations intended to guide the deceased through the Duat and ensure their successful transition to the afterlife.
sources.title: The Egyptian Book of the Dead

sources.name: The Amduat
sources.description: An important funerary text detailing Ra's nightly journey through the twelve hours of the Duat, highlighting the challenges and deities encountered.
sources.title: The Amduat

sources.name: The Book of Gates
sources.description: A funerary text depicting the sun god Ra's journey through the Duat, emphasizing the gates he must pass through and the guardians he must overcome.
sources.title: The Book of Gates

sources.name: Pyramid Texts
sources.description: The oldest known religious texts from ancient Egypt, inscribed on the walls of pyramids, providing early descriptions of the afterlife and the role of the pharaoh.
sources.title: Pyramid Texts

sources.name: Budge, E.A. Wallis. *The Gods of the Egyptians*
sources.description: A comprehensive overview of Egyptian deities and mythology, including detailed descriptions of the Duat and its significance.
sources.title: Budge, E.A. Wallis. *The Gods of the Egyptians*
